display.title: The Nigrescent Beyond: Mexico, the United States, and the Psychic Vanishing of Blackness
display.abstract: What place does Mexico deserve in Black Studies? It is a question that few concerned with the African diaspora ask, but, as Ricardo A. Wilson II argues in The Nigrescent Beyond, more of us should. While it is currently fashionable to interrogate liberal racial politics, Wilson challenges us to consider Mexico’s unique racial ontologies and their place in the African diaspora as we do so. “What in the critical practices generated by black studies,” he asks in reference to Paul Gilroy’s The Black Atlantic (1993) and Orlando Patterson’s Slavery and Social Death (1982), “has compromised its vision of this possibility?” (21). Wilson concludes that we must look beyond the Anglophone world—and to Mexico—to find alternative Black futures and to break free of the antiblackness inherent to Western modernity.Before 1640, Mexico was one of the primary destinations for the transatlantic slave trade. Racial mixture, which developed in part as a result of this history, birthed a colonial caste system that Mexicans rejected in the name of liberal equality under the law after their independence in 1821. This nineteenth-century project engendered what Wilson calls “psychic vanishing” (4). As a nation that officially embraced what we might today call post-racialism in the 1820s, Mexico provides Wilson with two centuries worth of literary, historical, and archeological sources on which to theorize the significations and resonances of Blackness and antiblackness historically and in the present. Wilson frames Mexican calls for race blindness as a legalist strategy that “privileges erasure over a permanent record” (28). They are a form of archive-building that until recently has rendered modern Mexican culture and society beyond the empirical, methodological, and theoretical contours of the African diaspora.Through the theory of psychic vanishing, Wilson explores not only how Blackness vanished in Mexico’s national imaginary but also how Mexicans unconsciously passed down these racial assumptions for generations, a practice that he laments is becoming more frequent in the United States. The Nigrescent Beyond challenges readers to look beyond the tropes of racial (in)visibility and racial disappearance, which itself has been a defining feature of Mexican nation-state formation. These paradigms, Wilson contends, too easily send Black Studies into facile discussions of racial repression and diatribes about diasporic authenticity. Instead, a focus on psychic vanishing allows scholars “to sharpen an understanding of the question, How, in this contemporary moment, can a text be read to tell the story of something that has to have no story?” (4–5). The goal, Wilson cautions, cannot be to recover, retrieve, or excavate lost racialized truths, for such seemingly noble quests are impossible in an antiblack world where some dimensions of Blackness are irretrievable.After introducing the main arguments in the introduction, The Nigrescent Beyond follows a loose chronology from the enslavement of Africans, through abolition, to the present. Chapter One explores how, at the end of the sixteenth century, the free Black community, San Lorenzo de los Negros, established by Gaspar Yanga in Veracruz, Mexico, created a Black radicalism not present in the annals of U.S. history. Chapter Two analyzes several twentieth-century accounts and transcriptions of Mexico City’s 1692 grain riot to explore how psychic vanishing transpired at the expense of the radical Blackness detailed in Chapter One. Moving to the United States, Chapter Three traces how psychic vanishing emerged in the historical and cultural commemorations of Abraham Lincoln, like Steven Spielberg’s 2012 film Lincoln. It explores how liberal discourse denied African Americans, like Frederick Douglass, historical agency while it transformed Lincoln into a paragon of anti-racist theory and practice whose neatly curated legacy was ready for Barack Obama to inherit in 2008. The connections between Mexico and the United States solidify in Chapter Four when Wilson turns to the vexing archeological question of whether Mexico’s pre-Columbian Olmec heads are of African origin. Unpacking the narratives penned, for example, by Matthew Stirling, Miguel Covarrubias, and Ivan Van Sertima, he follows these debates in and across U.S., Mexican, and Afro-diasporic circles. Not in search of an answer to this question—for that, Wilson contends, would be impossible and irresponsible—he uses it to ruminate on the nature of psychic vanishing and to elucidate his own methodology about the fragmentation of knowledge and the loss of empirical evidence endemic in Black Studies. In directly comparing contemporary Mexico with the United States, Chapter Five details Mexican attempts to recognize the nation’s African heritage, including on the 2020 census, and the United States’ current desire to hide the violence inflicted on Black bodies and, therefore, to undercut Black radicalism. Built around an analysis of Colson Whitehead’s novel Zone One (2011), the afterword looks to the future as it ponders how a racial utopia can be attained in spite of the racial conundrums Wilson elucidates in the first five chapters.Aware of the stir The Nigrescent Beyond may create, Wilson acknowledges that it is “a work of theoretically oriented speculative fiction” (101). Yet in his exhortations to orient Black Studies in the longue durée of Mexican psychic vanishing, his south-to-north narrative gets lost in theories emanating out of the Anglophone world and those that have been translated into English. Mexican constructions on Blackness operate primarily as appendages to his readings of Nicolas Abraham and Maria Torok’s analysis of psychoanalysis in The Shell and the Kernel (1987; translated from French by Nicholas T. Rand in 1994), Erin Graff Zivin’s an-archaeological methodologies, and Stephen Hawking’s hypotheses about black holes, among others. For example, despite acknowledging that the 1946 publication of anthropologist Gonzalo Aguirre Beltrán’s La población negra de México, 1519–1810 has been hailed as a “commemorative moment” (3) in Afro-Mexican Studies, this book (and others by Aguirre Beltrán) serve as mere reference points for Wilson’s analyses of other Mexican texts. Without greater analysis of the foundational texts in the field and without better historical and cultural contextualization of the sources he chose to discuss, it is not always clear if Wilson has chosen the most representative sources about Blackness in Mexico.Despite these critiques, The Nigrescent Beyond broadens the geographic and theoretical boundaries of Black Studies. Wilson shines in showing how racial vanishing occurs dialogically, for instance, through the translation and transcription of texts. By interrogating how narratives are researched, told, and disseminated, The Nigrescent Beyond requires nuanced reading, presupposes some historical knowledge of Mexico and the United States, and raises important questions about racial essentialism. Scholars of indigeneity, especially of pre-Columbian Mexico, may find the ways in which Wilson avoids the scholarly consensus about the Olmec heads (that they are not of African origin) troubling, but they are not his target audience. But to dwell on this misses his larger point about Black Studies and U.S. racial politics today. “If Afrofuturism remains focused on how to unsettle a future that wishes to disappear us,” he writes in the afterword, “it is imperative for us to examine how a multidecade (sometimes blind) devotion to an archaeological mind-set has compromised the range of our visions. In doing so, I believe we in black studies must ask ourselves how the irrecoverable has been inherited in a way that Afrofuturism and similarly entangled frameworks cannot unsettle” (107). To free ourselves of this quixotic search to exhume and resuscitate a true Black sensibility might be liberating. Wilson certainly thinks it will be. It will be up to the rest of us to follow through with greater empirical analysis and to see what future this methodology makes.

marc.value: The Nigrescent Beyond: Mexico, the United States, and the Psychic Vanishing of Blackness
